title: Where Does e Come From? Compound Interest & the Self-Derivative

md: Every textbook hands you the number
and tells you it's the natural base for logarithms and exponentials. **But where does this strange number come from?** And why does it earn the title "natural"?
Thompson's *Calculus Made Easy* gives the cleanest answer there is — using **money**. We follow it here, then connect it to calculus.
Suppose you put \$100 in a bank that pays **10% interest per year**. After 1 year, you have \$110. After 10 years — how much?
**Case (a): Simple interest.** Each year you withdraw your \$10 of interest and put it under your mattress. After 10 years: $100 + 10 \cdot 10 = 200$. Doubled in 10 years.
**Case (b): Compound interest, once a year.** Leave the interest in the account; let it earn interest too. After 1 year: \$110. After 2: \$121. After 3: \$133.10. After 10 years:
More! Because the interest itself starts earning interest.

blocks.title: What if we compound more often?
blocks.body:
The bank could be **even more generous**: pay 5% every half-year instead of 10% once a year. After 10 years that's 20 compounding periods of 5%:

$100 \cdot (1.05)^{20} \approx \$265.33$

More generous still — quarterly (40 periods of 2.5%): $100 \cdot (1.025)^{40} \approx \$268.51$.

Monthly (120 periods of 5/6%): $100 \cdot (1 + 0.1/12)^{120} \approx \$270.70$.

Daily (3650 periods): about \$271.79.

Every second? Continuously? **Where does this end?**

md: Splitting the 10% interest into $n$ equal periods, applied $n$ times per year, for 10 years gives:
And the limit as $m \to \infty$ is *exactly the number $e$*:
**So:** \$100 compounded continuously at 10% per year for 10 years becomes $100 \cdot e \approx \$271.83$.

blocks.title: The defining property of e
blocks.body:
Forget the 10%. The general statement: $e$ is the multiplier you get when **a quantity grows by its full size after one unit of time, but pays you back continuously** — every infinitesimal moment its growth-rate is proportional to its current size.

Thompson calls this the **law of organic growth**: growth where the **increment is proportional to the current magnitude**. Bacteria multiplying, radioactive decay, money compounding, populations exploding, hot tea cooling — anywhere growth rate scales with current size, $e$ shows up.

md: Define $e^x$ as the value of \$1 grown continuously at 100% per unit time for $x$ units of time. Then:
Expanding with the binomial theorem and letting $n \to \infty$ gives Thompson's beautiful series:
Now **differentiate this series term-by-term** using the power rule:

blocks.title: The most-loved fact in calculus
blocks.body:
$\dfrac{d(e^x)}{dx} = e^x$

The function $e^x$ is its **own** derivative. It is the *only* function (up to a constant multiplier) with this property. That's what makes $e$ uniquely 'the' base — every other exponential has a derivative that's $e^x \cdot$ (some constant).

md: Here is the same fact stated in plain growth language: **at every instant, $e^x$ is increasing at a rate exactly equal to its current value**. The bigger it gets, the faster it grows — proportional to itself. That's organic growth, and $e^x$ is its mathematical embodiment.
**Natural log** $\ln x$ is the inverse of $e^x$. If $y = \ln x$, then $e^y = x$.
Differentiate both sides of $e^y = x$ with respect to $x$. The left side needs the chain rule:

blocks.title: The 'curious' result
blocks.body:
Thompson points out a beautiful oddity: $\dfrac{1}{x} = x^{-1}$ is the **one power of $x$** that the power rule's antiderivative recipe can't handle. The rule $\int x^n \, dx = \dfrac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$ explodes when $n = -1$ (you'd divide by zero).

So the answer to "what function has derivative $\dfrac{1}{x}$?" had to come from a DIFFERENT direction — from the natural log. That's $\int \dfrac{1}{x}\, dx = \ln|x| + C$.

It's a beautiful symmetry: the one place the power rule fails is exactly where the logarithm steps in to fill the gap.

md: ## 5. The full picture, in one table

The last two formulas have $\ln a$ poking out because any other base $a$ is "unnatural" — it carries an extra conversion factor compared to base $e$. That's the meaning of "natural" in *natural log*: the only base for which the derivatives are clean.

blocks.title: Why this matters — outside the textbook
blocks.body:
Anywhere a quantity changes at a rate proportional to its current size, $e^x$ governs the math:

- **Compound interest** (the original example)
- **Population growth** — small bacterial colonies double on a clock, governed by $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$
- **Radioactive decay** — atoms decay at a rate proportional to how many are left
- **Cooling/heating** (Newton's law of cooling)
- **Charging/discharging a capacitor** — RC circuits
- **Drug elimination** from the bloodstream
- **Inflation, depreciation** in economics

All of these are *the same equation*, just dressed up: $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = k\,y$, solved by $y = y_0 e^{kt}$. That equation is so common it has its own name: the **differential equation of exponential growth and decay**, the simplest non-trivial differential equation in the world.
